items.title: ETDRS Classification
items.contentHtml:
Classification according to ETDRS

  • ETDRS (Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study) classifies into NPDR (non-proliferative) mild, moderate, severe and very severe, and PDR (proliferative) with or without high-risk features.
  • Severe NPDR: 4-2-1 rule: hemorrhages/MA in 4 quadrants, venous beading in ≥2 quadrants, IRMA in ≥1 quadrant.
  • Very severe NPDR: ≥2 of the severe criteria.
  • High-risk PDR: disc neovascularization (NVD) ≥1/3 of the disc area, or neovascularization elsewhere (NVE) ≥1/2 of the disc area. disc with vitreous/preretinal hemorrhage.

items.foco: Glycosylated hemoglobin as a risk factor
items.title: HbA1c as a risk factor
items.contentHtml:
Glycosylated hemoglobin as a risk factor

  • HbA1c is the main modifiable risk factor for the development and progression of diabetic retinopathy.
  • The DCCT (Diabetes Control and Complications Trial) study demonstrated that reducing HbA1c to <7% reduces the risk of type 1 retinopathy in 76%.
  • In type 2, UKPDS showed benefit with HbA1c <7%.
  • Every 1% decrease in HbA1c reduces the risk of retinopathy by ~35-40%.
  • Trap: abrupt decrease in HbA1c can temporarily worsen retinopathy (phenomenon worsening).

items.title: Anti-VEGF in the DR
items.contentHtml:
Treatment with anti-VEGF

  • Main indication: diabetic macular edema (DME) with foveal involvement and proliferative diabetic retinopathy (PDR) as an alternative to panretinal photocoagulation.
  • Approved drugs: ranibizumab (Lucentis, 0.3 mg/0.05 mL), aflibercept (Eylea, 2 mg/0.05 mL), bevacizumab (Avastin, 1.25 mg/0.05 mL, off-label).
  • Regimen: initial monthly dose, then as needed (PRN) or treat-and-extend.
  • DRCR.net Protocol T study: aflibercept superior to ranibizumab and bevacizumab in initial visual acuity <20/50.
  • Adverse effects: endophthalmitis, retinal detachment, increased intraocular pressure.

items.title: diabetic macular edema
items.contentHtml:
Diabetic macular edema

  • Defined as retinal thickening within 1 disc diameter of the center of the macula.
  • Classification: DME with central involvement (foveal) vs without central involvement.
  • Diagnosis by OCT (optical coherence tomography) showing thickening and intraretinal cysts.
  • First-line treatment: intravitreal anti-VEGF; intravitreal corticosteroids (triamcinolone, dexamethasone implant) as a second line.
  • Trap: DME can occur in any stage of DR, not only in PDR.
  • Risk factor: elevated HbA1c, hypertension, dyslipidemia.

items.title: Panretinal photocoagulation
items.contentHtml:
Panretinal photocoagulation

  • Indicated in high-risk PDR (NVD ≥1/3 of the disc or NVE with vitreous hemorrhage) and in severe NPDR with poor glycemic control or lack of follow-up.
  • Objective: ablation of peripheral ischemic retina to reduce VEGF production.
  • Technique: 1200-1600 burns of 200-500 µm, separated by 1 burn diameter, in the peripheral retina, sparing the macula and papilla.
  • Adverse effects: loss of peripheral vision, worsening of macular edema, nyctalopia, exudative retinal detachment.
  • The DRS study (Diabetic Retinopathy Study) demonstrated reduction of severe visual loss in high-risk PDR.

items.title: Vitreous hemorrhage
items.contentHtml:
Vitreous hemorrhage

  • Most common cause: rupture of neovessels in PDR, due to vitreous traction.
  • Presentation: sudden painless loss of vision, myodesopsias, vision of reddish fog.
  • Diagnosis: B-scan ultrasound if dense hemorrhage prevents seeing the fundus; rule out associated retinal detachment.
  • Initial management: rest, control of HbA1c and BP; if it does not clear in 4-6 weeks, consider pars plana vitrectomy.
  • Indications for early vitrectomy: dense vitreous hemorrhage that does not clear, presence of tractional retinal detachment, hemolytic glaucoma.

items.title: Tractional DR
items.contentHtml:
Tractional retinal detachment

  • Complication of advanced PDR due to contraction of fibrovascular membranes at the vitreous-retinal interface.
  • Mechanism: tangential and anteroposterior traction, elevating retina without tear.
  • Symptoms: progressive decrease in vision, metamorphopsias, central scotoma if affected macula.
  • Diagnosis: OCT and B-scan ultrasound show elevated retina with traction.
  • Surgical treatment: pars plana vitrectomy with membrane dissection and endolaser; if it involves the macula, urgent surgery.
  • Trap: it can be combined with retinal tear (tractional-rhegmatogenous DR).

items.title: Initial microaneurysms
items.contentHtml:
Microaneurysms as initial finding

  • First clinical sign of diabetic retinopathy, visible as small red dots in the retina (usually in the posterior pole).
  • Pathophysiology: weakness of the capillary wall due to loss of pericytes and thickening of the basement membrane.
  • On fluorescein angiography they appear as dots. hyperfluorescent with late leakage.
  • Typical location: inner and outer plexiform layers, with greater density in the temporal macular region.
  • Differential diagnosis: punctate hemorrhages (red dots that do not hyperfluorescent in angiography).
  • Its isolated presence defines mild NPDR.

items.title: NVD
items.contentHtml:
Neovascularization of the optic disc

  • Definition: neovascularization in or within 1 disc diameter of the edge of the optic nerve.
  • Marker of high-risk PDR according to DRS if it occupies ≥1/3 of the disc area.
  • Pathophysiology: severe retinal ischemia releases VEGF stimulating growth of abnormal vessels in the papilla.
  • Risk of massive vitreous hemorrhage and tractional retinal detachment.
  • Treatment: panretinal photocoagulation or first-line intravitreal anti-VEGF.
  • Snare: NVD may be difficult to detect with vitreous hemorrhage; use fluorescein angiography to confirm.

items.title: PDR
items.contentHtml:
Proliferative diabetic retinopathy

  • Defined by the presence of retinal or disc neovascularization, or vitreous/preretinal hemorrhage.
  • Advanced stage of DR, with high risk of severe visual loss.
  • Risk factors: Elevated HbA1c, hypertension, duration of diabetes, pregnancy, nephropathy.
  • Treatment: panretinal photocoagulation (historical standard) or anti-VEGF (ranibizumab, aflibercept) as an alternative.
  • DRCR.net Protocol S study: ranibizumab non-inferior to PRP for PDR, with less loss of peripheral visual field.
  • Complications: vitreous hemorrhage, detachment traction retina, neovascular glaucoma.

items.title: Fluorescein angiography
items.contentHtml:
Fluorescein angiography

  • Imaging technique with intravenous contrast (sodium fluorescein) to evaluate retinal microvasculature.
  • Phases: choroidal (10-15 s), arterial, arteriovenous, venous (30 s - 2 min), late (5-10 min).
  • Findings in DR: microaneurysms such as hyperfluorescent spots, leak in macular edema, neovascularization with progressive leak, areas of non-perfusion (hypofluorescence).
  • Indications: evaluate clinically significant macular edema, guide photocoagulation, differentiate neovessels from IRMA.
  • Contraindications: allergy to fluorescein, severe asthma, renal failure (creatinine clearance <30 mL/min).

items.title: BP control in DR
items.contentHtml:
Strict control of blood pressure

  • Hypertension accelerates the progression of diabetic retinopathy, independently of HbA1c.
  • UKPDS study showed that reducing BP to <150/85 mmHg decreased progression of DR and need for photocoagulation.
  • Current guidelines recommend target <130/80 mmHg in patients with diabetes and retinopathy.
  • Drugs of choice: ACEI or ARA-II due to renoprotective effect independent of BP reduction.
  • Trap: sudden decrease in BP can worsen retinal perfusion in severe ischemia.
  • Monitoring: ambulatory measurement of PA (MAP) to confirm control.
